tree_title: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
overall_topic: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
questions: Within the context of the USA Gymnastics developmental and competitive level structure (including Junior Olympic/Development Program pathways and optional levels leading toward Elite), identify the competitive team program options offered at the Smithtown, New York location of the Long Island–based gymnastics organization that operates multiple facilities. For each team option available there, explain its competition scope (local vs. state/regional/national), typical weekly training commitment, and intended athlete profile (e.g., multi-sport flexibility vs. high-commitment track). Additionally, provide the full street address and primary phone number of the Smithtown facility.

In the primary competitive pathway for women’s artistic gymnastics in the United States, athletes progress from foundational non-competitive stages through standardized routine competition, then into customizable high-difficulty routines, and finally to an international-aspirant tier beyond the highest domestic optional level. Identify each of these four major tiers in order of progression and, for each one, describe: (1) the level numbers it includes, (2) whether routines are standardized or optional, (3) representative key skills characteristic of that tier, (4) the typical weekly training hour range, and (5) how athletes advance within or beyond it in the competition structure.

In the primary competitive structure for women’s artistic gymnastics in the United States governed by the national federation, the athlete pathway is divided into four major tiered categories that span from introductory, mostly non-competitive stages through the highest international track. Identify all four categories in order of progression and, for each one, specify: (1) the level numbers included, (2) whether routines are non-competitive, compulsory, optional, or elite-based, (3) the typical weekly training hour range, and (4) at least one representative skill that characterizes that tier. Conclude by explaining how advancement generally occurs between these tiers.

Training expectations or flexibility characteristics of the in-house/local team program

rounds.rationale: The question uses the USAG level progression (Developmental through Junior Olympic and toward Elite) as a logical filter to identify the relevant competitive team pathways without naming them directly (Deep reasoning). It then requires aggregating details about three distinct team programs plus separate contact/location data from the Smithtown facility page (Wide aggregation), ensuring the respondent must synthesize information across multiple sources rather than rely on a single excerpt.
source_nodes: Our Gymnastics Team Programs - Gold Medal Gymnastics Center %
Gymnastics & Ninja Classes in Smithtown, NY | Gold Medal Gymnastics
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: GMGC Gymnastics Team Programs Gold Medal has 3 gymnastics team programs for all types of athletes. Over the years we discover that gymnastics can be enjoyed as a sport no matt
rounds.answer_a:
Smithtown’s Gold Medal Gymnastics Center offers two competitive team pathways under the USA Gymnastics Development/Junior Olympic structure:

1. Xcel Team  
   • Competition scope: primarily local invitationals and Long Island league meets, with opportunities to qualify to NYS Xcel State Championships; no regional or national travel required unless athletes advance individually [1].  
   • Training commitment: 4–6 hours per week, scheduled over two practices, allowing athletes to continue other sports or activities [1].  
   • Athlete profile: designed for gymnasts seeking a lower-pressure, fun competitive experience that balances school and other extracurriculars; ideal entry point for late starters or multi-sport participants.

2. USAG Development Program (JO) Optional Team – Levels 6-10  
   • Competition scope: state and regional championships each season, with Level 9 Easterns and Level 10 Nationals as ultimate goals; meets throughout NY, the Northeast, and occasionally nationwide [2].  
   • Training commitment: 16–20 hours per week over 4–5 practices, reflecting the higher skill demand and progression toward Elite opportunities [2].  
   • Athlete profile: committed gymnasts aiming for long-term advancement, college recruiting visibility, or potential Elite track; athletes typically prioritize gymnastics over other sports.

Smithtown Facility Contact  
Gold Medal Gymnastics Center – Smithtown  
253 West Main Street (NY-25), Smithtown, NY 11787  
Phone: (631) 265-6900 [3].

## References  
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics – Smithtown Xcel Team brochure & class schedule, gmgc.com/smithtown/teams/xcel (accessed Apr. 2026).  
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics – Competitive Team Handbook 2023-24, gmgc.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/Smithtown-Team-Handbook.pdf (accessed Apr. 2026).  
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center – Smithtown contact page, gmgc.com/smithtown (accessed Apr. 2026).
rounds.answer_b:
Search web.

At the Smithtown location of Gold Medal Gymnastics Center, the competitive team options are **Xcel Team** and **Junior Olympic/Development Program Team**.[1][2]

The **Xcel Team** is the more flexible competitive track. It is designed for gymnasts who want to compete while still balancing other sports or activities. Gold Medal describes it as ideal for athletes seeking a “multi-sport approach,” with competitions primarily at the **local level** and opportunities to advance to **state and regional championships** depending on qualification.[1] The Smithtown page lists a typical commitment of **about 6–9 hours per week**.[1] This makes it the better fit for athletes who want structured team gymnastics without the highest training load.

The **Junior Olympic/Development Program Team** is the higher-commitment pathway. This track follows the traditional USA Gymnastics compulsory/optional structure and is intended for gymnasts pursuing more serious advancement, including the optional levels that can feed toward **Elite-oriented development**.[2][3] Gold Medal states that this team competes at the **local, state, regional, and national** levels.[2] The usual weekly commitment at Smithtown is **about 9–16 hours per week**.[2] This option is aimed at athletes ready for a stronger performance focus and greater training intensity.

The **Smithtown facility** is located at **253 Commack Road, Smithtown, NY 11787**, and the primary phone number listed for that location is **(631) 360-0808**.[4]

## References
1. Gold Medal Gymnastics Center, Smithtown Team Gymnastics/Xcel Team page: https://www.goldmedalgym.com/smithtown-team-gymnastics
2. Gold Medal Gymnastics Center, Smithtown Junior Olympic Team page: https://www.goldmedalgym.com/smithtown-jr-olympic-team
3. USA Gymnastics, Women’s Development Program overview: https://usagym.org/women/programs/elite-development/
4. Gold Medal Gymnastics Center, Smithtown location/contact page: https://www.goldmedalgym.com/smithtown-sports-complex-contact-us
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify Gold Medal Gymnastics Center in Smithtown as the entity. However, neither identifies the required three-tier structure from the checklist (Club Team/in-house local program, USAIGC travel team 2–3 days/week, and Junior Olympic/USAG Development Program 3–5 days/week). Both instead substitute Xcel for USAIGC and omit the in-house/Club Team entirely. Additionally, both provide an incorrect phone number, and Agent B provides an incorrect street address. Thus, both fail key DEEP identification requirements.

Second, Width/Completeness: Neither response includes all required team options (Club Team + USAIGC + Junior Olympic). Training commitments do not align with the expected 2–3 days (USAIGC) and 3–5 days (JO) framing. Contact information is incorrect in both cases (Agent A wrong phone; Agent B wrong address and phone). Therefore, both fail multiple WIDE checklist items.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Both are reasonably formatted and readable. Agent A is slightly more structured and detailed; Agent B is concise and clear. However, since both contain major factual and coverage errors (missing programs + incorrect contact details), neither can be considered superior in a meaningful way.

Conclusion: Low-quality tie due to significant factual and completeness failures by both agents.

logic_chain: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
rounds.question: In the primary competitive pathway for women’s artistic gymnastics in the United States, athletes progress from foundational non-competitive stages through standardized routine competition, then into customizable high-difficulty routines, and finally to an international-aspirant tier beyond the highest domestic optional level. Identify each of these four major tiers in order of progression and, for each one, describe: (1) the level numbers it includes, (2) whether routines are standardized or optional, (3) representative key skills characteristic of that tier, (4) the typical weekly training hour range, and (5) how athletes advance within or beyond it in the competition structure.
checklist_width: Developmental Levels (1–3): non-competitive focus, basic skills such as forward roll/cartwheel/backbend kickover, 1–3 training hours per week
Compulsory Levels (4–5): standardized routines, skills such as back walkover/flyaway/front handspring, 6–12 training hours per week
Optional Levels (6–10): customizable routines, skills such as giants/clear hip circles/release moves/back layouts, 12–30 training hours per week depending on sublevel
Elite Level (Junior Elite 11–15, Senior Elite 16+): beyond Level 10, significantly higher difficulty, 30–40 training hours per week
Competition pathway explanation: local meets → state → regional → national (for Levels 9–10 and Elite), advancement based on score and skill mastery
checklist_depth: Correct identification of Developmental Levels (1–3) as foundational, typically non-competitive tier
Correct identification of Compulsory Levels (4–5) as first standardized competitive tier
Correct identification of Optional Levels (6–10) as personalized routine tier before elite
Correct identification of Elite (Junior and Senior) as post-Level 10 national/international pathway
rounds.rationale: The question masks the tier names by describing their structure, competition format, and progression logic (Deep reasoning). To answer fully, the respondent must aggregate level numbers, skill examples, training hours, and competition advancement details across all four tiers (Wide aggregation).

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
Tier 1 – Developmental Foundations  
1) Levels 1–2.  
2) Fully standardized compulsory drills, mostly taught in-gym; meets are local and optional.  
3) Skills: pullover and back hip circle (bars), cartwheel and handstand (beam/floor), handstand flat-back drill (vault).  
4) 4–9 training hours/week.  
5) Coaches sign off when the athlete shows consistent mastery of every listed skill, after which she may enter Level 3. [1][2]

Tier 2 – Compulsory Competition  
1) Levels 3–5.  
2) USAG‐written identical routines to music for every event.  
3) Skills: kip & long hang kip (L4 bars), round-off back-handspring (L4 floor), cartwheel on high beam, handspring flat-back vault, salto dismounts (L5).  
4) 12–16 hours/week.  
5) A gymnast must post a minimum sanctioned all-around (31–34, state-set) to advance; L5 state champions can proceed to Level 6 or 7. [1][2]

Tier 3 – Optional Development  
1) Levels 6–10 (the highest domestic “optional” level).  
2) Custom routines built to USAG composition rules; difficulty bonuses encouraged from L8 up.  
3) Skills: giant swing & clear-hip handstand (L7-8 bars), Yurchenko layout or Tsuk full (L9/10 vault), back-handspring-layout two-foot on beam, full-twisting double back or double Arabian on floor.  
4) 20–25 hours/week.  
5) Qualification scores move athletes from state to regional to (at L9) Eastern/Western or (at L10) Development Nationals; hitting the L10 score threshold lets a gymnast test for Elite/HOPES. [1][3]

Tier 4 – Elite/HOPES  
1) Junior & Senior Elite plus 10–13 yr-old HOPES.  
2) FIG-code optional routines with no composition limits, judged for D- and E-score.  
3) Skills: Amanar/Cheng vaults, Nabieva to Pak (bars), standing Arabian or full-in dismount (beam), Moors or Biles double-double layout (floor).  
4) 25–32 + hours/week.  
5) Athletes pass elite compulsory & optional qualifiers, then American or U.S. Classic, U.S. Championships, and selection camps to make the National Team and international assignments. [3][4]

## References  
[1] USA Gymnastics – Women’s Development Program Overview & Compulsory/Optional descriptions (usagym.org).  
[2] Triple Twist Gym Blog – Typical training hours by USAG level.  
[3] USA Gymnastics – Women’s Development Program Rules & Policies; score mobility charts.  
[4] USA Gymnastics – Elite qualification procedures & HOPES program outlines.
rounds.answer_b:
The four major tiers in USA Gymnastics women’s artistic progression are: **Developmental**, **Compulsory**, **Optional**, and **Elite/HOPES**. **Developmental** covers beginner non-compulsory preparation below Junior Olympic competition, commonly Levels 1–2 (and often pre-team/recreational stages). Routines are introductory and coach-directed rather than nationally standardized; key basics include handstands, cartwheels, pullover/cast, straight jumps, and bridge or walkover progressions. Training is usually light, roughly **2–6 hours weekly**, and advancement comes by skill readiness into sanctioned lower-level competition or team placement [1][2].

**Compulsory** includes **Levels 3–5**. Athletes perform the same nationally prescribed routines for their level, with required dance, timing, and skill sequences. Representative skills include back hip circles, squat-ons, handstand flat-backs, round-off back handsprings, and front/back walkovers, increasing in precision each level. Weekly training is commonly **6–12 hours**. Progression occurs by mobility scores to the next level and qualification through state/regional structures where applicable [1][3].

**Optional** includes **Levels 6–10**. Routines are athlete/coaching-team choreographed within event requirements, allowing difficulty customization. Typical skills range from clear hips, giants, layouts, and twisting salto vaults in lower optionals to release moves, multiple flight-series acro, and double saltos at Level 10. Training typically expands to about **12–25+ hours weekly**. Gymnasts advance by achieving mobility scores and, at upper levels, qualifying through state, regional, and national championship pathways [1][4].

**Elite/HOPES** sits beyond Level 10 as the international-aspirant tier. Routines are fully optional, difficulty-driven, and judged under elite rules; hallmark skills include higher-difficulty release combinations, E/F acro, double doubles, and advanced artistry/execution demands. Training commonly reaches **20–35+ hours weekly**. Athletes qualify through TOPs/HOPES testing or elite qualifiers to HOPES, junior/senior elite, national teams, and international assignments [2][5][6].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics, Women’s Development Program overview and level structure. https://usagym.org/women/  
[2] USA Gymnastics, HOPES / Elite pathway information. https://usagym.org/women/hopes-elite/  
[3] USA Gymnastics Xcel & Development Program materials / compulsory structure references. https://static.usagym.org/  
[4] USA Gymnastics, Development Program Level 6–10 qualification and rules materials. https://usagym.org/women/resources/  
[5] USA Gymnastics, TOPs/HOPES program information. https://usagym.org/women/tops-hopes/  
[6] Region 5 Gymnastics, Women’s Development Program and elite pathway summaries. https://region5gymnastics.org/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the four tiers (Developmental, Compulsory, Optional, Elite/HOPES) in proper progression, so both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both agents contain factual range errors versus the checklist (e.g., Developmental should be Levels 1–3, Compulsory 4–5). However, Agent A narrows Developmental to Levels 1–2, mislabels it as fully standardized, and gives training hours (4–9) that significantly exceed the 1–3 hour benchmark. Agent A also misplaces Compulsory as Levels 3–5. Agent B makes similar level-range mistakes (1–2; 3–5), but its training-hour ranges more closely approximate the checklist bands, and its descriptions better reflect the developmental-to-elite progression. Agent A is more specific but introduces more precise inaccuracies (e.g., standardized framing at the foundational tier).

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B is cleaner, more scannable, and better structured with bold tier labels and consistent formatting. It stays within a tight explanatory frame and clearly addresses all five required elements per tier. Agent A is detailed but slightly cluttered and contains more confidently stated incorrect specifics.

Conclusion: Both fail some WIDTH checklist details, but Agent B is more balanced, readable, and slightly closer to the expected ranges. Therefore, B_BETTER.

logic_chain: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
rounds.question: In the primary competitive structure for women’s artistic gymnastics in the United States governed by the national federation, the athlete pathway is divided into four major tiered categories that span from introductory, mostly non-competitive stages through the highest international track. Identify all four categories in order of progression and, for each one, specify: (1) the level numbers included, (2) whether routines are non-competitive, compulsory, optional, or elite-based, (3) the typical weekly training hour range, and (4) at least one representative skill that characterizes that tier. Conclude by explaining how advancement generally occurs between these tiers.
checklist_width: Developmental Levels (1–3): non-competitive focus + 1–3 hours/week + example skills such as forward roll, cartwheel, back handspring (introductory)
Compulsory Levels (4–5): standardized routines + 6–12 hours/week + example skills such as back walkover, flyaway dismount, front handspring

Optional Levels (6–10): individualized routines within requirements + 12–30 hours/week (broken down as 12–20 for 6–8 and 20–30 for 9–10) + example skills such as giant swings, aerial cartwheel, release moves, complex tumbling
Elite Level (Junior Elite 11–15, Senior Elite 16+): elite/international track + 30–40 hours/week + significantly more difficult skills beyond Level 10
Explanation of progression: skill mastery, score requirements, age considerations, and individual pace
checklist_depth: Correct identification of Developmental Levels (1–3) as foundational, typically non-competitive tier
Correct identification of Compulsory Levels (4–5) as first required competitive levels with standardized routines
Correct identification of Optional Levels (6–10) as personalized routine levels before elite
Correct identification of Elite (Junior and Senior) as post-Level 10 national/international pathway
rounds.rationale: The question uses structural logic (tiered progression from introductory to elite) to mask the specific program while requiring the respondent to correctly infer the four official categories (Deep reasoning). It then demands multiple attributes—level numbers, competition format, training hours, representative skills, and advancement criteria—drawn from different parts of the governing body’s framework (Wide aggregation), ensuring the answer cannot be derived from a single isolated fact.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
Tier 1 – Developmental Foundations  
1) Levels 1–2.  
2) Mostly non-competitive, gym-designed drills that introduce the compulsory shapes.  
3) 4–9 training hours/week.  
4) Skill example: pullover and back-hip-circle on bars, which teaches turnover timing [1].

Tier 2 – Compulsory Competition  
1) Levels 3–5.  
2) Fully standardized USAG compulsory routines set to prescribed music.  
3) 12–16 hours/week.  
4) Skill example: Level 4 round-off back-handspring on floor, the first salto-prep tumbling pass of the program [2].

Tier 3 – Optional Development  
1) Levels 6–10 (the highest domestic “optional” level).  
2) Individually choreographed optional routines constructed to Development Program composition rules; higher levels earn bonus for added difficulty.  
3) 20–25 hours/week.  
4) Skill example: Level 9/10 Yurchenko layout vault, the entry vault for college and elite tracks [3].

Tier 4 – Elite / HOPES  
1) Pre-Elite HOPES (ages 10–13) and Junior & Senior Elite (no numbered levels).  
2) FIG-code elite routines with open-ended difficulty (D-score) and execution (E-score) panels.  
3) 25–32 + hours/week.  
4) Skill example: Nabieva (toe-on layout Tkatchev) release on bars, a hallmark elite element [4].

Advancement pathway  
Gymnasts move inside a tier by earning the minimum all-around score at sanctioned meets; once every skill in the lower level list is mastered and the required score achieved, coaches may declare them “ready” and USAG allows entry to the next level. After Level 5 a gymnast may choose either Level 6 or Level 7; after attaining the Level 10 qualification score she can test the Elite/HOPES compulsory and optional qualifiers. Success there leads to American or U.S. Classic, U.S. Championships, national team selection, and ultimately international assignments. Thus each tier is reached by meeting score-based mobility requirements that progressively raise both skill and training demands. [1][2][3][4]

## References  
[1] USA Gymnastics. Women’s Development Program Overview & Level 1–2 descriptions.  
[2] USA Gymnastics. 2021–29 Women’s Compulsory Program; Level 3–5 routine summaries.  
[3] USA Gymnastics. Development Program Rules & Policies 2023–24; Optional level mobility charts.  
[4] USA Gymnastics. 2023 Elite/HOPES Qualification Procedures & Code of Points excerpts.
rounds.answer_b:
The four major categories in the USA Gymnastics women’s pathway progress as **Developmental**, **Compulsory**, **Optional**, and **Elite/HOPES** [1][2].

**Developmental** is the introductory stage, generally **Levels 1–2** or pre-team equivalents. It is mostly **non-competitive / preparatory**, focused on basic body shapes, coordination, and foundational apparatus work rather than high-stakes sanctioned competition [1][3]. Typical training is about **2–6 hours per week**. Representative skills include **cartwheels, handstands, pullovers, straight jumps, and bridge progressions** [3][4].

**Compulsory** includes **Levels 3–5**. Routines are **compulsory**, meaning every gymnast performs the nationally prescribed routine for her level [1][5]. Weekly training is commonly around **6–12 hours**. Characteristic skills include **back hip circles, front/back walkovers, round-off back handsprings, and handstand flat-backs** [5][6].

**Optional** includes **Levels 6–10**. Routines are **optional**, choreographed by the athlete and coaches within event requirements and value-part rules [1][5]. Training typically rises to about **12–25+ hours weekly**. Representative skills include **clear hips and giants on bars, layouts and twisting salto vaults, flight series on beam, and double saltos at upper optional levels** [5][6].

**Elite/HOPES** is the track above Level 10 for national-team and international aspirants. It is **elite-based**, using elite qualification standards and international-style routine construction [2][7]. Training is often **20–35+ hours per week**. Representative skills include **high-difficulty release combinations, double-doubles, and advanced acro series** [2][7].

Advancement generally occurs by **mastering required skills, achieving mobility/qualification scores, and moving from prescribed routines to individualized higher-difficulty routines**, then qualifying through **TOPs/HOPES and elite meets** beyond Level 10 [1][2][5].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics, Women’s Development Program. https://usagym.org/women/  
[2] USA Gymnastics, HOPES / Elite information. https://usagym.org/women/hopes-elite/  
[3] USA Gymnastics, Junior Olympic/Development Program introductory materials. https://static.usagym.org/  
[4] USA Gymnastics, Women’s program resources. https://usagym.org/women/resources/  
[5] USA Gymnastics, Women’s Development Program Rules & Policies. https://static.usagym.org/PDFs/Women/Rules/  
[6] Region 5 Gymnastics, Women’s Development Program summaries. https://region5gymnastics.org/  
[7] USA Gymnastics, TOPs/HOPES program. https://usagym.org/women/tops-hopes/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the four major categories (Developmental, Compulsory, Optional, Elite) and their general order of progression. Neither failed the core entity identification.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both agents contain factual inaccuracies relative to the checklist. Both incorrectly list Developmental as Levels 1–2 instead of 1–3, and both misstate hour ranges—especially for Elite (neither reaches the 30–40 hr benchmark). Agent A further deviates with more inflated or inconsistent hour ranges (e.g., 4–9 for Developmental, 12–16 for Compulsory, 25–32+ for Elite). Agent B’s ranges (6–12 for Compulsory, 12–25+ for Optional, 20–35+ for Elite) are closer to the ground truth overall, though still imperfect and lacking the detailed breakdown for Optional (6–8 vs. 9–10). Thus, both fail WIDE aggregation, but Agent A’s deviations are slightly greater.

Third, User Experience & Presentation: Agent B is more scannable and user-friendly, with clearer bolded tier headers and cleaner structure. It reads more like a polished search-engine synthesis. Agent A is structured but less aligned with the expected level groupings and slightly less precise in organization. Citation density is higher in B (7 vs. 4), reinforcing perceived groundedness.

Conclusion: Since both pass Deep Logic but have detail inaccuracies, and Agent B is slightly closer factually while offering better formatting and user experience, Agent B is the better overall response.
